articles.title: Odisha invokes ESMA in power sector, bans strikes for six months amid heatwave and supply concerns - ODISHA RAY
articles.full_text:
Odisha invokes ESMA in power sector, bans strikes for six months amid heatwave and supply concerns
Bhubaneswar(23/05/2026) By Ishani Chhatoraj : In a significant move aimed at ensuring uninterrupted electricity services across Odisha, the state government has invoked the Essential Services Maintenance Act (ESMA) in the power generation, transmission and distribution sector, prohibiting strikes and work stoppages by employees and associated personnel for the next six months.The notification, issued by the government, declared services related to electricity generation, transmission, distribution, load dispatch operations, grid management and maintenance as “essential services” under the provisions of ESMA.The order came into immediate effect and will remain in force for a period of six months.Officials said the decision was taken in view of the prevailing extreme heatwave conditions, rising power demand and the need to maintain uninterrupted electricity supply across Odisha.The government expressed concern that any disruption in power services could severely affect public life, healthcare facilities, water supply systems, industries and essential infrastructure.Under the ESMA provisions, employees, contractual workers, outsourcing personnel and organisations associated with the power sector have been barred from participating in strikes, demonstrations leading to disruption of services, mass leave or any activity that may hamper electricity operations.The order empowers the government to take disciplinary and legal action against individuals or groups found violating the directive.Authorities stated that any disruption in electricity services during the enforcement period would be treated as a violation of essential service regulations and could invite penal action under the law.Sources in the Energy Department indicated that the move was also prompted by concerns over possible labour unrest and operational challenges in the power sector at a time when electricity consumption has surged sharply due to intense summer conditions.Several parts of Odisha have been witnessing temperatures crossing 40 degrees Celsius, leading to increased demand for cooling appliances and continuous power supply.The government maintained that uninterrupted electricity services are critical for maintaining public order and ensuring the functioning of hospitals, transportation systems, communication networks and drinking water supply projects, especially during emergencies and extreme weather conditions.The invocation of ESMA has drawn mixed reactions from employee unions and industry observers.While some stakeholders viewed the decision as necessary to safeguard public interest during the peak summer season, certain labour representatives expressed concern over restrictions on workers’ rights to protest and raise service-related grievances.Odisha has witnessed mounting pressure on the power infrastructure in recent weeks amid recurring heatwave conditions and complaints of power outages in several districts.The State government has repeatedly directed power utilities and distribution companies to strengthen infrastructure, improve response mechanisms and ensure uninterrupted supply during the summer months.Officials said the administration will closely monitor the implementation of the ESMA order and coordinate with power utilities to maintain stable electricity services across the state.

articles.title: Public fury mounts over frequent power cuts during heatwave conditions in Odisha - Odisha TV
articles.full_text:
Frequent power outages amid intense heat and high humidity triggered protests in several parts of Odisha. Residents alleged prolonged disruptions in electricity supply and inadequate response to repeated complaints.
As temperature remain elevated across the state, consumers reported electricity interruptions lasting several hours. In some areas, residents staged demonstrations wearing saris or carrying handheld fans, while road blockades were also reported in protest against the outages.
Consumers Question Summer Preparedness
Electricity Consumers' Association president Ramesh Satpathy questioned the preparedness of authorities to deal with increased summer demand.
"Every year, power cuts occur during the summer and the explanation given is that the system tripped due to increased load. Did the authorities not know that electricity consumption would rise during the summer months? Why were adequate preparations not made in advance? The present government made several promises, but people continue to face difficulties," he said.
Referring to the privatisation of power distribution, Satpathy added, "The four discoms were handed over to Tata Power on the premise that services would improve. However, consumers are facing increasing problems after Tata Power took charge of all four discoms."
Concerns Over Impact On Poultry Farms
Satpathy also raised concerns about the impact of power cuts on commercial activities, particularly poultry farming.
"When consumers approach officials seeking uninterrupted supply for households and businesses, they are often told that nothing can be done. Poultry farms are incurring losses because of power disruptions during this heatwave. There is also insufficient availability of diesel to operate backup systems. This is happening at a time when the government is speaking about self-sufficiency despite shortages in poultry and egg production," he said.
He further alleged, "The Odisha Electricity Regulatory Commission had stated that Rs 20,000 crore was invested under various schemes. However, there is no visible record of such expenditure improving the situation. Demand increases during summer every year, so the present circumstances should not come as a surprise."
Satpathy also argued that management of all four distribution companies by a single private operator should be reviewed.
"It was not appropriate to hand over all four discoms to one company. The responsibility should have been distributed among different operators," he said.
Analyst Flags Supply Management Issues
Odisha power analyst Ananda Mohapatra claimed that electricity availability remains a concern despite the ongoing demand.
"The situation across Odisha has become difficult for consumers who are unable to manage daily life without electricity. The government and GRIDCO are not procuring 500 MW of power available from companies such as Vedanta and Jindal during this period of scarcity," he said.
Mohapatra further alleged, "The distribution companies are not seeking the additional 500 MW from the State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC), and the SLDC is not allocating it either. To manage the shortfall, power supply is being curtailed at various locations."

The allegations come amid continuing public concern over electricity supply during the ongoing spell of hot and humid weather across Odisha.
